name: SVG
description: SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) is an XML-based vector image format developed by the W3C. Unlike raster formats (PNG, JPEG), SVG describes images as geometric shapes, paths, and text, allowing them to scale to any size without losing quality. This makes SVG the ideal format for logos, icons, illustrations, charts, and UI graphics that need to look sharp at any resolution — from mobile screens to large displays and print. SVG files are human-readable XML and can be styled with CSS or manipulated with JavaScript.
technicalDetails: SVG is an XML vocabulary that encodes geometry (paths, polygons, circles, etc.), transforms, fills, strokes, gradients, filters, and text. Browsers render SVG natively via their layout engine. SVG files can be embedded inline in HTML, referenced as an img src, or used as CSS backgrounds, each with different capabilities for scripting and styling.
commonUses: Animated graphics via CSS or JavaScript
advantages: Small file sizes for vector artwork
Styleable with CSS and scriptable with JavaScript
disadvantages: Not suitable for photographs or complex raster imagery
Complex SVGs with many paths can be slow to render
Some advanced features have inconsistent browser support
Requires vector source artwork — raster images cannot be losslessly converted to SVG

faq.question: What is SVG and why should I use it?
faq.answer: SVG is a vector format, meaning images are stored as mathematical descriptions of shapes rather than pixels. This makes SVG graphics infinitely scalable without blurring, and typically smaller than raster equivalents for logos, icons, and simple illustrations.

faq.question: Can I convert a photo to SVG?
faq.answer: Not meaningfully in most cases. SVG is a vector format and photos are raster data. Automatic tracing tools can approximate a photo as vector paths, but the result is typically a poor likeness and a very large file. SVG is best authored from scratch in a vector editor.

faq.question: Is SVG safe to use on the web?
faq.answer: SVG is safe when served as a static file or referenced via an img tag, which prevents script execution. Be careful with inline SVG from untrusted sources, as it can execute embedded JavaScript. Always sanitize user-supplied SVGs before rendering them inline.
